name: Chest-Supported Rear Delt Row
resistance_profile: Chest-supported vertical row with high, flared elbows; steady gravitational load through a horizontal-abduction-biased pull, strongest near the contracted top.
cues: Elbows high and wide — drive them toward the ceiling.
Lead with the elbows; the hands just hold on.
Pull to the line of your shoulders, not toward your hips.
execution_steps: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and lie chest-down with a dumbbell in each hand hanging straight below the shoulders.
Start with the arms extended and the shoulder blades relaxed forward.
Row the dumbbells up and out with the elbows flared to roughly 75-90 degrees from the torso — a wide, high path.
Pull until the upper arms are in line with the shoulders and squeeze the rear delts and mid-back.
Lower under control to a full stretch and repeat without heaving off the bench.
good_when: You want to load the rear delts heavier than a fly allows while staying strict.
You want combined rear-delt and mid-back development in one movement.
You want a lower-back-friendly, chest-supported pull.
bad_when: You keep the elbows low, which turns it into a lat/upper-back row.
You want pure single-joint isolation — choose a rear-delt fly instead.
common_errors: Tucking the elbows so the lats take over.
Heaving the chest off the bench for momentum.
Loading so heavy the biceps curl the weight up.
progressions: Add load once strict, high-elbow form holds.
Add a peak-contraction pause at the top of each rep.
regressions: Reduce the load or raise the bench angle.
Switch to a bent-over dumbbell rear delt fly.
